media_note: Launch — AI strategy vs usage graph

post_text:
Every company bought the AI license.
Half are barely running inference.

A thread on the AI usage gap 🧵

1/ The usage gap ≠ cancellation.

It's the company that:
• Still pays for the platform
• Still lists AI in the annual report
• Quietly stopped calling the API

Then budget season hits and everyone acts confused.

2/ Licensed spend is the *budget line*.

Run-rate usage is what teams actually consume.

When usage < spend, you have shelfware with momentum problems — not a technology problem.

3/ Signals worth watching in AI ops:

→ Inference decay (month over month)
→ Usage ratio (actual / licensed)
→ Active seats vs provisioned
→ Who owns the workflow after pilot ends
→ Whether the legal entity still exists 🚨

4/ Real pattern from our demo data:

Media company. $520K AI platform.
Inference: 100 → 54 in 6 months.
Internal team built a replacement workflow.
Nobody updated the ops map.

Verdict: fading adoption. Not a bug report.

5/ The fix isn't another pilot.

It's usage telemetry + identity checks + an AI loop that commits to a read with numbers — not vibes.

We built it at murmur.red

RT if your AI dashboard looks healthier than your inference graph.

📊 AI usage telemetry (demo portfolio, real math):

Licensed spend:  $2.5M
Actual usage:    $2.11M run-rate
Idle gap:        $390K
Fading:          2 of 5

15.6% of your AI budget might be ambient noise.

Hot take: the AI rollout isn't failing in the demo.

It's failing in week 9 — when the pilot team moves on, the API key stays active, and nobody checks the dashboard.

Usage telemetry > launch announcement.

Drop the AI tool your company bought but barely uses.

I'll start: enterprise platform, -46% inference over 6 months, internal replacement built quietly. Dashboard still green. 💀

Shipped: generator-critic loop on usage data.

Claude reads the numbers → critic attacks the take → revise with evidence.

No more 'looks healthy' without citing inference, MoM %, or day count.

Your inference bill is a honesty meter.

Spike = people found a workflow that works.
Flatline = shelfware with a logo on it.

Tokens don't care about your press release.

The 90-day pattern:

If inference drops >15% while the contract still runs,
the tool didn't fail — the workflow did.

Catch it in ops review. Not at the board slide.

What's the first sign AI adoption is fading?

🔴 Inference decay
🟡 Workflow owner left
🟢 Active users dropping
⚫ 'We're still exploring use cases'

Plot twist: the company didn't cancel AI.

They just stopped calling it.

Contract still active ✅
Dashboard still green ✅
Inference down 46% over 6 months ❌

Different problem. Different fix.

Most AI rollouts die after the demo video.

Here's the ops playbook that keeps inference alive 🧵

1/ Stop measuring launches.
Start measuring inference.

If monthly API calls trend negative for 3+ months, the workflow lost — regardless of what the survey said.

2/ The 85% usage rule.

Actual consumption / licensed spend < 0.85?
Something changed in how the company runs AI.
Find out what before the next budget cycle.

3/ Reorg = adoption risk.

No new owner for the AI workflow within 30 days?
Inference decay accelerates.
Assign an ops owner + business sponsor immediately.

4/ One action per company. Not a roadmap.

Owner + measurable outcome + deadline.
'Map which team still calls the API' beats 'drive AI adoption' every time.

5/ We run a generator-critic loop on usage data:

Generate read → critic attacks → revise with evidence.

No vague 'low engagement.' Only numbers.

Live simulator: murmur.red

MoM inference velocity: -2% sounds tiny.

Over 6 months on a $520K AI platform?
That's a quarter-million in usage that evaporated.
Procurement still shows full contract.

Paper vs reality.

Build vs buy AI is the wrong frame.

The real question: build vs buy vs *actually operate*.

Most companies nail procurement.
Almost nobody owns the ops layer after go-live.

We cross-check every company against public registries.

Identity health for B2B AI contracts.
Because sometimes the org paying for inference doesn't legally exist anymore.

🇳🇱 Registry data > gut feel.

Ops people — what do you wish leadership actually measured on AI?

(Mine: inference trend. Not slide count.)

Honest question for ops leaders:

Does your AI spend match actual usage?

📊 Yes — we track inference
😬 Sometimes — we catch it late
💀 No idea — we track the contract

🏭 Company story (demo data, anonymized):

Media company. Big AI platform contract.
This is what fading adoption actually looks like 🧵

Month 1: $520K contract. 100 inference units. 100 seats.
Month 6: 54 units. 41 active users.

Ops dashboard: 🟢 fine
Support volume: high
Workflow owner: gone

The team didn't quit AI loudly.
They just... routed around it.

Internal tool absorbed the workflow.
API calls dropped. Nobody updated the runbook.

Registry check: legal entity flagged dissolved.

Contract still running.
Inference still decaying.
Budget line still full.

Three dashboards. Three stories.

The lesson:

Buying AI ≠ operating AI.
Watch inference. Watch ownership. Watch identity.
